content.problemStatement:
Chef has a list of intervals intervalListintervalListintervalList, where each interval is represented by a start and an end value. 

Chef wants to merge all overlapping intervals so that the resulting list contains only non-overlapping intervals covering all the ranges from the original list. 

Help Chef by writing a function that takes the list of intervals and returns the merged intervals sorted by their start values.

Function DeclarationFunction NamemergeIntervalsmergeIntervalsmergeIntervals - This function merges all overlapping intervals in the given list and returns the merged intervals.

Parameters
intervalListintervalListintervalList: A reference to a list of intervals, where each interval is represented as a array of two integers [start,end][start, end][start,end].
Each interval represents a numeric range, and the list may contain overlapping intervals.
Return Value
Returns a array of array containing the merged intervals.
Overlapping intervals are combined into single continuous intervals without overlaps.
Constraints
1≤intervalList.size()≤1041 \leq \text{intervalList.size()} \leq 10^41≤intervalList.size()≤104
Each interval contains exactly 2 integers.
0≤intervalList[i][0]≤intervalList[i][1]≤1040 \leq \text{intervalList}[i][0] \leq \text{intervalList}[i][1] \leq 10^40≤intervalList[i][0]≤intervalList[i][1]≤104

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
Each test case contains:

First line: An integer NNN — the number of intervals Chef has.
Next NNN lines: Each line contains two integers representing the start and end of an interval.




Output Format

For each test case, print the merged intervals in a single line.
Each merged interval should be printed as two integers separated by a space.
Print all merged intervals sorted by their start values, separated by spaces.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the number of intervals. The next N lines each contain two integers start and end (0 ≤ start ≤ end ≤ 10^4) describing an interval.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line. Print all merged, non‑overlapping intervals in increasing order of start. Each interval must be printed as "[l r]" (including the brackets) and intervals are separated by a single space. End each test case with a newline.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1 merges overlapping intervals into [1 5] and [6 10]. Test case 2 merges all three consecutive intervals into [0 3]. Test case 3 after sorting merges [5 7],[6 10],[8 9],[10 12] into [5 12] while [1 4] stays separate.
First test case merges [2 4] with [4 6] → [2 6] and [7 9] with [8 10] → [7 10]; the last interval remains. Second test case has no overlaps.

solution.approach: For each test case sort the intervals by their start value. Then scan once, keeping the current merged interval [curL,curR]. If the next interval starts <= curR, extend curR to max(curR, next.end). Otherwise output the current merged interval and start a new one. This greedy scan produces the minimal set of non‑overlapping intervals covering the same range.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case for sorting, O(N) for the linear scan.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the intervals (in‑place sorting can be used, but auxiliary space for the result is O(N) in the worst case).
